mod 6 - Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 4
Speaker 1: Hey guys. Welcome to module six. So at this point in the training you've launched your
product and you've generated momentum right out of the gate with a powerful cart
open e-mail and maybe you even got ambitious and added a fast action cart open bonus
like we also spoke about in module five. Even though this makes for a really great start,
it's by no means time to take your foot off the accelerator 'cause the truth is most
people won't buy the moment you open up your cart. It remains true that no matter
how well you executed the first part of your launch, most of your sales are still going to
come during the sales and closing sequences.
Now before we go deeper you might be worrying why I just separated those two. What
is the difference between a sales and closing sequence? I mean most marketers and
copywriters just kind of lump that in in the same general category as followup e-mails
but in this course, especially when we're trying to coach the conversion, there is a
distinction to be made and a pretty important one at that. On the surface here is the
difference. The sales sequence will start right after cart open and conclude 48 hours
before the close at which point your closing sequence will clock in for duty and do its job
to maximize convergents right up until the final buzzer.
This distinction is necessary because while they play similar roles there are some crucial
differences. It's just kind of like how some types of e-mails will perform much better
during the close than they would the sales sequence and vice versa. Part of what we're
doing in coaching the conversion is identifying and really tapping into what kind of
messages our audience will resonate with and which ones they need to hear at what
time in what order in order to move closer to, and eventually beyond, that buyer's line.
One of the best ways probably to kind of illustrate the difference between the two is
how whereas in a closing sequence you'll definitely wanna be leveraging and using time
urgency, leading with it when the clock still has more than two days remaining is
actually counterproductive. That's a major mistake that I see a lot of marketers make is
you'll get those e-mails or messages that say, "four days left until we take it away", "72
hours left". All that's really doing is it's not creating urgency, it's coaching complacency
and complacency is obviously not what we're going for here.
Now the other big mistake that comes up a lot in the sales sequence is marketers will
make the mistake of diluting their sales messages by adding new ones. A lot of the times
we feel that if someone didn't buy right out of the gate it means they weren't
responding to our initial set of beliefs, reframes, and sales arguments which is not the
case, or at least you can't full know that until your launch is complete and you do a full
post-mortem. You really do need to allow your sales sequence and your closing
sequence to do their jobs and to reinforce those sales arguments and, of course, the
closing sequence to coach that decisiveness before making any kind of assessment of
whether or not your sales arguments, and the way you presented them, and the beliefs
and attitudes and shifts all of that were appropriate or not. So that's another big
mistake. The sales sequence isn't the time to throw more beliefs, outcomes, and sales
arguments against the wall to see what sticks. It's time to double down on the ones
you've already made.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 4 --

mod 6 - Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 4
We do this by really reinforcing those arguments with proof, with case studies. Just
backing them up in a stronger way, building an irrefutable case. Another way we do this
in a very necessary way is those beliefs are now re-contextualized with your offer, with
your product as the mechanism that catalyzes it, that really leverages those beliefs
you've instilled. Because you believe X product Y is what now makes sense for you. So
we are taking those beliefs they've already bought into and re-contextualizing them
with your product.
Now it's important to say before we go deeper into this that no matter how well you
execute your sales sequence, statistically speaking it's still going to be the slowest part
of your launch but that doesn't mean it's not important. It doesn't mean we can just
kind of mail it in and send a few e-mails and call it a day because done well what we're
still doing here is we're chipping away. We're setting up the close. This is our
opportunity to reinforce all the groundwork we've done in that prelaunch instead of
diluting it of course. Then from there we can lean on a powerful closing sequence that
will really help your prospect down that final leg of that journey.
So if the job of the closing sequence is to pull your prospect across, what is this
marketing dead zone between launch and 48 hours remaining meant to accomplish?
What are we actually coaching here? There are four things. The first thing is
identification with being a buyer. For one, we really need our prospect at this point in
our launch to start seeing themselves as a buyer, to identify in a very real, grounded,
and practical way the similarities between them and other people who have raised their
hand and said, "Yes, I do want to complete this journey." When we get into the
themeplates here you're gonna see one called the upgraded FAQ which is a really great
way to coach that identification. Another one you're gonna see is one that you could use
for your case studies called The Curious Case. That one is going to be a unique way of
presenting a case study in a way that really draws in curiosity and also elicits that
identification with a buyer, either your prospect seeing a case study and saying, "That's
so me", or seeing that case study and saying, "That's exactly who I want to be and I feel I
can become that person through your product".
Moving on, we're also coaching recommitment and the closing of the consistency loop.
Because we reflected back intent in the confirmation series and reaffirmed commitment
to those outcomes, this is where we get to cash in on that. This is where we get to close
that consistency loop and to really remind them, remind our prospect, of the outcomes
they've committed to and gently hold them accountable to it. This is where we're
closing that consistency loop. We close it in the sales sequence and then we tighten it
even more in the closing sequence.
Another thing we're coaching is the risk revealer. Many of us have heard of something
called risk reversal, which you'll see more of in the closing sequence. It's definitely
something we still use but its lesser known corollary is what I call risk revealer, sorry.
What this is is it's just bringing your prospect closer, more intimate to the stakes of
going backwards. It's reminding them that stepping back is going back into that burning
house, that the old way has a greater cost, more friction than moving forward. So that is
the risk we are revealing. To really coach this and really ground it and instill it in your
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 4 --

mod 6 - Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 4
prospect we're gonna explore two themeplates on this one. We're gonna have The Point
of No Return and The Blind Eye.
Then finally, last but not least as we eluded to earlier in this lesson, this module, this
sequence is really coaching reinforcement of beliefs, reframes, and outcomes with your
product as the conduit, as the thing or mechanism that delivers it. So it's not giving
them a million other new things to think about. That's actually one of the things that will
stop people from buying is when you confuse them, when you give them too many
things to think about that gets in the way of decisiveness. So we're not going to be doing
that. Instead, we're going to be giving them a new context of which those beliefs now
exist. It exists within the context of your product. That's the final thing. Just don't dilute
your sales messages and reframes. Instead, reinforce them. Pad it with proof from your
case studies, personal anecdotes into how your product syncs up with this new vision
and is in line with all these beliefs and these outcomes and these realities that you've
created together.
That's what this sequence is selling. Once again, it's not about putting on surface level
urgency and warning them that there's 72 hours left because that is nothing but a
reprieve. That is just coaching complacency. However, there is going to be a little
conditional thing that I want you to consider adding here, especially if you're doing an
extended launch where your sales sequence could last three or four or even seven days.
I highly recommend adding a mid-cart bonus which will add some legitimate urgency as
well as really help kind of overcome that dreaded lull that can happen between your
initial cart open and your cart close.
For these mid-cart bonuses I've worked with clients who've used a wide array and
different types of them really. The ones that I recommend, the ones I've seen get the
best results, is I mean first of all something that helps them immediately after the
course, or using our terminology, something that after completing our course will
exclusively empower them to use that next thing. So for example, if you were selling a
course on creating courses, your mid-cart bonus might be a small training on how to sell
it. That would be the immediate next step. If you were teaching a beginner guitar player
how to play the essential chords, your bonus might include a training on how to start
combining them, how to transition between those chords so that you can play songs.
What this does is on a subtle yet powerful level it presupposes a successful outcome. It
gets your prospect thinking of what's beyond and the availability of new and exciting
options and outcomes that exist for them after they achieve what your course or
program has promised. That's very powerful. It presupposes success. It presupposes
that they're gonna be in that training and accomplish the outcome that you've been
promising them.
Now a second option is actually gonna tie in really, really well with your case study.
You're gonna see how to do that in the The Curious Case themeplate. The point here is
to really show an exciting process or result that you spoke about or that a testimonial or
previous client outlined in the case study and then introduce a bonus that supports that
further. So it supports your core material in generating that specific outcome from the
case study. For example, let's say our case study was about a guitar player who after just
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 4 --

mod 6 - Main Lesson (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 4 of 4
three weeks was able to play Green Day's Time of Your Life around the campfire which
made him feel awesome. You can include a special bonus that breaks down the exact
technique of how to play that song really fast, how to learn how to play that song really
fast and speed that up.
Those are the two mid-cart bonuses that I see work really well. I would recommend
starting with the first one, something that would really help them after they get the
main promise, after they've completed the main part of your course 'cause that
presupposes the successful outcome. The second one, if you are using a case study and
if that case study has a certain outcome that could be further supported beyond what's
in your core training then I would really recommend including a bonus that just
complements your core training in getting them that result. So with all that said, I know
there was a lot in this module but we are gonna take a break and then we are gonna
explore these themeplates in greater detail which will show you how to activate these
principles and really coach them in a practical way through your e-mail messaging. So
let's take a break and I will meet you in the workbook.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
